speaker: interviewer
text: And any time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Date-- May 6, 1996. Survivor-- Ellen Kerry Davis. Maiden name-- Wertheim. Interviewer-- Helene Elkus. Cincinnati, Ohio. United States of America. Language-- English.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any time.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is bright. Oh, OK, can I-- was that on? Oh, OK. My name is Helene Elkus. I am conducting this interview with Ellen Kerry Davis. It's May 6, 1996. We're in Cincinnati, Ohio, United States of America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell us your name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Ellen Kerry Davis, spelled E-L-L-E-N K-E-R-R-Y D-A-V-I-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name at birth? And spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wertheim, W-E-R-T-H-E-I-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age?

speaker: interviewer
text: The city and country of birth? And please spell the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, I come from a-- I was born in a village, which was Hoof, H-O-O-F near Kassel, K-A-S-S-E-L in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your family life in Hoof?

speaker: interviewee
text: That depends how far you want me to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, as far back as you feel comfortable in telling what is part of your-- your background.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well my background changed immensely. I can go back to the time I was three, when it was a very happy time. And then I can go to the age of four, when the happiness came to an end.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll start-- let's start the happy background first, then.

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived with grandparents, and uncles, and my parents, of course, and yeah, one younger brother. And it was just a very content, normal-- very normal sort of Jewish household. We were orthodox, but we were used to that, because we knew nothing different. My grandmother was a joy. She was a little ball of a woman, almost as broad as she was tall and always strikes me as white, black and white. She had snow white hair and always a black dress and a white pinafore. And my grandfather was twice as tall as she was and always looked strict. But my memories of him were at the back of the house there was a garden with a stream in it. And my happiest memories, if it could be happy, is that almost daily he would come down to the stream and drag me out of this because it was my favorite place. And this was just a happy family. And then life changed dramatically.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well what was your father like and your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a very gentle person, soft and gentle, as I remember her. My father was a very masculine man, whose hobbies were bodybuilding and things of that description, which were really not known then, but that was his hobby. He was tough, even to us children. But he could be gentle. But the relationship sort of changed, again, as I got older and as the years went by.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do for an occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: He--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it on? OK. Can you please tell us what your father did?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father worked with my grandfather, who was a butcher. And he was the [NON-ENGLISH], the Jewish butcher. And my uncles and my father worked with him. So he was-- again, one big family. It was a family business and we just lived as one big family. Outside the home the children played together. We had friends of all-- we never even thought of denomination. Nobody said, you're a Jew or you're not. It just never occurred to us. And it was just a completely normal childhood, which-- I had two brothers by the time I was three. And I had no dolls. I just looked after babies, which came very naturally to me. But it was normal. It was a joyful one. During the day, most of the time we played. On the Shabbos we went to shul-- of course, grandfather herding everybody. And I remember things like the meals my grandmother prepared for Shabbos and our Shabbos [NON-ENGLISH], for instance, who would come in and switch on lights, and put wood on the fire, and things like that-- and wonderful, wonderful memories like Pesach, things that stay in one's mind. Pesach was very-- very special to me. I remember this very long room, which actually was two rooms. They had taken the doors down. And Opa, my grandfather, was sitting in this chair, surrounded by pillows, because this is how it's supposed to be. And all my aunts and uncles-- and the room was full. And it was full of joy. And Pesach was a great occasion because the family was together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back a lot of generations in this village or in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we went back to the 1700s, which is quite something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I only discovered that later, of course. This is all hindsight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain what was the first sign of the changing when Hitler came into power? When did you start to feel a change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very-- very easily I can explain that [INAUDIBLE]. First  of all, all the children we used to play with either ignored us, or threw stones at us, or just called us Jew. Well, for a child who doesn't know what the difference is between a Christian and Jew except that they went to a church, we went to a synagogue-- and it hurt because we'd been friends. And I was only four years old. And the main memory was the fact that my father's best friend was the village policeman. And I remember, being a very nosy child, coming down one night hearing voices. And there was the village policeman and my father-- the policeman who we called uncle, saying to my father, Julius, don't talk to me in the street because I can't talk to you. If I shout at you, forgive me. Tell the children they mustn't call me uncle. Because, he said, I have to wear this dreadful uniform. I have a family. And I have to live here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for the first time in my young life, I discovered that men could cry. I never knew that-- I knew I could cry when I was hurt, but for men to cry? That I didn't know. And that was the greatest shock of all, the fact that these two big, strong men could cry. And then I realized why, when he said, tell the children that they mustn't call me uncle, and if I shout at them, tell them, please, take no notice. What was his-- do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. I was four years old. Names don't come easily. And these are things that stick in one's memory, but names never have. For me, names are something I don't remember. But it was-- it was hard because, as I said, I was only four years old. And all these little friends who one took for granted suddenly turned into little enemies. And it continued that way, until virtually I went to school, which was even more horrific. But my father had-- as I said, he was a bodybuilder. And he had very-- not strange ideas, but he had different ideas. And because I was the eldest, he thought my best defense would be attack. So at four, he taught me to fight, to box and physically fight.

speaker: interviewer
text: His reasoning was that I was the eldest and I had to look after the young ones. And therefore, I had to learn to fight, which is precisely what happened. When we were attacked-- usually by a bunch, rarely by a single child, usually by a bunch-- I would come home with a split lip or a black eye. And my father would turn round and say to me, what did the other one look like? And I guarantee you they were much worse than I. And you watched-- you took care of your siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took care of my siblings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us the names of your siblings and how many there were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. One a year younger than I was Rolf. And the one two years younger than I was Heinz. And then four years younger was Sally. Five years younger was Ruth. And seven years younger was Ludwig, who was a babe in arms.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, we didn't get your mother's name, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my mother's name was Johanna. But she was always known as Hanna. And my father, of course, was Julius.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you did go-- did you go to the public school eventually and it was a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a little village school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I've been trying very hard to remember how many Jewish children, but I can't. But I went to school very excitedly because this was a great adventure, only to be put in the very back of the class with all the other Jewish children. And not only did the other children not speak to us, but the teachers didn't speak to us. So what we learned, we had to pick up, you know, factually. We had to listen. I never-- I didn't learn to write. I never learned to write because I was only in school for a year and a half. And then Jewish children were no longer allowed to go into non-Jewish schools. My brother joined me a year later. And he was only in school for six months. So then it was a case of-- by the time I was six, my grandparents had left to go to South America. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your grandparents leave? I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: How? They just-- one day they just went there. There was lot of talk about South America, about horses and a new life. And we were included in this. And unfortunately, it turned out there just wasn't enough money to include us-- my parents and my siblings and myself. So my [? Oma ?] and [? Opa ?] and three uncles and an aunt, and another uncle-- there was my sisters-- my sister-- my mother's sister eventually married my father's brother, who went with them to the Argentine. So they literally just vanished out of my life. And life, again, changed very dramatically, because I was then in charge of five children, which was tough, because not only did I have to look after them, I had to feed them, and wash their nappies, and do the cooking, and do all of the grownup jobs that a six-year-old should not have had to do. But I-- again, I took it for granted. It wasn't a hardship because I knew no better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there enough food for everyone after a while?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, this is a problem that's I can't-- I can't explain to you, because I can't explain to myself. The only way I can explain it is the fact that the community-- the Jewish community looked after their own. And I don't remember, at that time, going hungry. We had no new clothes, ever. Because I remember my father's sister, who was only two years older than me-- I had all her cast off clothing, which I detested. But there was no money. I mean, there was always food, even years before, but no money. You know, in an village, food is something that you take for granted and that you get. But [LAUGHS] I never had a coin in my hand. I never knew what money was for. So the Jewish community took care of us. And this is the only way I can work things out. Because I know my father couldn't work, because before my grandfather left, they had taken his business away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now they took your grandfather's business away--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --which was your father's business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which was also my father's. My father worked for my grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he had no means of making a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no means of making a living at all. And then we were still in this little village, where there were not even any prospects. But, as I said, this is the only way I can describe the fact that we ate. Because he brought in nothing in the way finances. He had no job. And therefore, there was no money. But I don't remember at that time being hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you afraid? Were you ever afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I think I was born without fear. Either that, or my father instilled into me that fear was no use to anybody. He made me aggressive rather than passive. And I've-- I have never, ever feared anything or anybody, not as one would normally fear. It hasn't actually been to my benefit, in some ways. As a child, most certainly, it benefited me. Because I had a lot to go through. But as an adult, I should have learned fear, because it makes one very careless when you have no fear.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a child, can you take us through some of the incidents and some of the things you experienced then? I mean, what was happening in the-- did you go to synagogue any more? What happened--

speaker: interviewee
text: We went--

speaker: interviewer
text: --to your religious life?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to synagogue for a little while, but only a little while. The next thing I remember is the fact that I must have been seven or eight. Chronologically, it is difficult. And I remember something I still have a horror of-- not a fear, horror of-- men in black leather coats coming to the house. My father was in Auschwitz. He just vanished for long--

speaker: interviewer
text: They took your father away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? Before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, long before that. He would go and come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: He could come and go from Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't understand it, except that he once explained, when I was much older, the fact-- I think the explanation is correct. They were, at that time, expanding Dachau, building more and more, and it could even be that they were already building the gas chambers. And I remember my father telling us that the commandant was a gambler. And I told you my father was very strong-- bodybuilding. And there was always a bet onto how many bricks a Jew could carry in a hod. And do you know what a hod is?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: A hod is a building thing. It goes like this. It's like a y-shape. But inside were all the bricks. And there would be two, my father and someone else. The commandant was not stupid and always bet on my father and always won. And each time my father won for him, he would get furlow. And I don't think this has ever happened to anybody before or since, because I have explained-- tried to explain this to people and they thought I was crazy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now was he in Auschwitz, you said, or Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dachau, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they were already building the other camps. But he was away, and these Gestapo came. And the first thing they wanted was my mother's jewelry. Now the only thing my mother had was a wedding ring and a tiny, tiny pair of coral earrings. And she had always said to me that these are going to be yours. And I remember them coming to the house, literally tearing off the ring from her finger and tearing the earrings out of her ears-- not taking them out, tearing them out. And a little later, they came back. They said, this house is too good for Jews. Out. We could take nothing. And again, the Jewish congregation made room for us, you know, in the school rooms of the synagogue. There were two rooms behind. And we all lived in one room. And there was a toilet and a washbasin. And someone had found a little cooker or whatever. And we lived behind the synagogue until 1937. I know Kristallnacht was in '38. But ours was in 1937. My father was home-- actually, every time my father was home, my mother was pregnant. This was one of those facts of life. And one eve-- one night in the middle of the winter, my father must have smelled smoke and went outside and saw the synagogue aflame, with us in it. So he managed to get all of us out-- and my mother, again, pregnant and a babe in arms. And there we could see a group of Nazi youths with bricks in their hands. They were determined to exterminate us as well as the synagogue. The synagogue burned down. And in the whatever it was, with the fires going and the people shouting, some Jewish-- some non-Jewish people got us away. And we were in our nightclothes and no shoes. And they hid us in an ice cellar, which in those days was a hollowed-out hill filled with ice. It was [INAUDIBLE] fridges. We were-- these people who lived in the big house put us into the ice cellar when there was virtually mass hysteria because they couldn't find us and they wanted to kill us. And we knew they wanted to kill us. But the rest of the day-- of that night is a complete blank. Actually, it was only four years ago that I saw this-- the ice cellar. And I met the daughter of the people who saved us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did you get-- how were you saved?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, in this mass hysteria they smuggled us away from the fire, these non-Jewish people, and they hid us in the ice cellar--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --until next day. And by next day, of course, the hysteria, which-- when a group of Hitler youths get together, they wind one another up, as we now say. And it's just one hysteric mob. And all they wanted to do was kill us. And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they kill anyone? Were there other people involved?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was they synagogue and us, nobody else involved. Five children two grown-ups-- that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you did get out, just--

speaker: interviewee
text: We got out, as I said, with the help of the people in the big house, whose name I don't remember. But as I said, I met their daughter again four years ago. And she remembered me. But I was seven years old-- I was eight years old. In 1937, I was eight years old. We didn't know what was what. We didn't know what to do. But again, as I said-- I can't say it often enough-- the Jewish community are so wonderful when it comes to disasters small or big. I think even then they knew that there but for the grace of God go I. Because it could have been their home, as it was a year later, when Kristallnacht came along and every other synagogue was burnt, whereas ours was just a year earlier.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you stay in this big house? I mean, what happened after you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, next day my father was sent back to Dachau. And my mother was sent to a camp for destitutes with the babe.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the little ones?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the babe in arms.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: And three brothers, a sister, and I were sent to the Jewish orphanage in Kassel. And we didn't see our father for a long time. And my eldest-- two eldest brothers and I-- because I was the eldest of all of them. So I was eight and they were seven and six. We used to be allowed to go, every Sunday-- well, one Sunday out of every four-- to go see our mother. But there was no transport. And we have to walk-- I can only now guess that it was five miles each way, just to see her for an hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was she-- what did they have her doing-- do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that takes a lot of explaining. They had these camps that were not just for Jews. Because there were gypsies there. There were homosexuals there. There were dissidents there. Before the concentration camps were actually for everybody. And these were camps that had been set up outside of towns for non-people, as we were known, non-humans. And it was a-- my mother, as everybody else, lived in one tiny room. And there was a stand pipe and one pipe of water and one toilet to every 50-- to every 50 units, as I can only call them, which were the rooms. My mother, I remember, very vividly-- because I didn't know what homosexuals were. She said these men were absolutely wonderful to her. Where she got food, I have no idea. What she ate, I have no idea. Because she just didn't tell us. All she wanted was to have her arms around us and know we are safe. And then we'd walk back the five miles, back to the-- what we called the [GERMAN], the orphanage in Kassel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dreadful. It's very difficult to describe. Because nowadays, when you speak of orphanages, there's all sweetness and light and everybody's looking after children. I'm going back nearly 60 years, when orphanages were-- I don't know if you've ever read any of Dickens, but he described them very beautifully. They were dreadful places. And you were just an entity. You never asked why. You just did what you were told which, in our case, was work, because no non-Jewish person would come in to clean or cook. So we, the children, had to do this ourselves. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a Jewish orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a Jewish orphanage, the only Jewish orphanage. And as I keep on saying, Jewish people are wonderful to their own. And there was no shortage of money in the orphanage. But by this time--
